title: Emergency Tarp and Dry-In Service in Spartanburg, SC | Commercial Roofers of Spartanburg
metaDescription: Emergency tarp and dry-in response for storm-damaged commercial roofs in Spartanburg County, stopping water intrusion until permanent repair is scheduled.

html:
Stopping Water Intrusion the Same Day It Starts

A damaged roof doesn't wait for a convenient repair window. When wind, hail, or a fallen limb opens a hole in a membrane, the priority shifts from a proper long-term fix to stopping water from reaching the deck, insulation, and whatever is stored or operating below.

What Emergency Dry-In Actually Involves

A dry-in isn't a single tarp thrown over a hole. It's a temporary but engineered barrier: reinforced poly sheeting or a fitted membrane patch, secured with battens or adhesive rated to hold against wind, sealed at every edge so water can't track underneath and spread beyond the original damage point.

On low-slope commercial roofs, a loose tarp that isn't properly secured can do more harm than the original damage by trapping water against the membrane or tearing loose in the next wind event and exposing a larger area than before.

Material selection matters too. A tarp rated for residential slope roofs isn't built for the standing exposure a flat commercial roof puts it through, so we use heavier reinforced sheeting designed to hold up under sustained wind and pooled water until the permanent repair goes in.

Response Priorities for Active Facilities

For manufacturing floors and distribution centers along the I-85 corridor, water reaching product, racking, or equipment is often the bigger cost than the roof damage itself. We prioritize dry-in work over roofed areas tied to active operations or inventory before moving to lower-priority sections of the same building.

Where a facility runs continuous shifts, we coordinate rooftop access with site security and operations so emergency crews aren't blocked from getting up when a call comes in overnight or on a weekend.

Plants running near BMW's assembly complex and the surrounding supplier base near Greer often have layered security clearance requirements for after-hours site access. We ask new emergency clients for that process ahead of time, rather than discovering it during an active call, so a crew isn't sitting at a gate while water is still coming through the roof.

Storm Patterns That Drive Emergency Calls

Upstate South Carolina sees its heaviest severe weather during spring and summer thunderstorm season, when straight-line wind and hail are the most common causes of sudden membrane damage. Falling limbs from mature trees around older properties in the mill villages and downtown Spartanburg are another recurring cause, particularly after a wind event loosens dead wood that comes down days later.

Aged built-up roofs already showing surface wear are more vulnerable to storm damage than newer single-ply systems, since brittle bitumen cracks under wind-driven debris impact where a more flexible membrane might just deflect it.

Rooftop mechanical units are also a common casualty during high wind events, and a unit knocked loose from its curb can leave a large open penetration that needs immediate attention alongside any membrane damage. We assess equipment condition as part of the same emergency visit rather than treating it as a separate call.

What an Emergency Response Includes

  • Same-day assessment of the damaged area
  • Temporary dry-in installation secured against wind
  • Photo documentation of damage before covering
  • Interior check for water intrusion already in progress
  • Written scope for the permanent repair to follow

From Temporary Protection to Permanent Repair

A dry-in is a bridge, not a fix. Once the immediate water intrusion risk is controlled, we schedule the permanent repair based on the extent of the damage and whether it affects a small section or points to broader membrane issues that need a fuller assessment.

For roofs still under manufacturer warranty, we document the storm damage in a format that supports a warranty or insurance claim, since the temporary repair itself doesn't affect eligibility as long as it's done correctly.

We also revisit every temporary dry-in on a regular check-in basis while it's in place, since a tarp that held during the initial storm can loosen over subsequent weather and needs periodic verification until the permanent repair is complete.

Coordinating With Insurance Documentation

Photos taken before and after the temporary dry-in are often the clearest record an adjuster has of the damage's original extent. We provide that documentation as part of the emergency response so the facility has it on hand regardless of when a claim gets filed.

Emergency Response Questions Facilities Ask

How fast can an emergency dry-in be scheduled?

Same-day in most cases, especially where active water intrusion is affecting operations or inventory below the damaged area.

Will a temporary tarp hold up in another storm?

A properly secured dry-in is built to hold through additional wind and rain until the permanent repair is scheduled, not only until the next dry day.

Does a temporary repair affect the roof's warranty?

Not when it's installed correctly and documented. We note the temporary repair in the file so it's clear to the manufacturer what was done and why.

How long can a roof stay under a temporary dry-in?

It depends on the extent of damage and the roof's overall condition, but we schedule the permanent fix as soon as materials and crew availability allow rather than leaving temporary protection in place longer than necessary, and we check on it periodically in the meantime.

Can you assess damage after hours or on a weekend?

Yes, storm damage doesn't wait for business hours and neither does the response. Crews are available for after-hours and weekend calls, particularly during peak thunderstorm season when most emergency calls come in.
